---
title: "Nestedness of the complexity matrix"
subtitle: "Do complex locations draw from a superset of the origins that feed simpler ones"
author: "Ollin D. Langle-Chimal"
date: today
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    html-math-method: katex
jupyter: python3
---

## Setup

The work location by education quantile matrix is binary once we mark, for each cell,
the education bins it draws from more than its size would predict. If the structure is
nested, the origins feeding a simple location are a subset of those feeding a complex
one. We measure this with NODF and compare it to a null that preserves the number of
active bins per location and per quantile.

```{python}
import os, sys
os.chdir("..")
sys.path.insert(0, "src")

import pandas as pd

from eci import load_panel, COUNTRIES, COUNTRY_NAME
from nestedness import binary_matrix, nodf_significance

rows = []
for c in COUNTRIES:
    b = binary_matrix(load_panel(c))
    s = nodf_significance(b, n_null=19, seed=1)
    s["country"] = COUNTRY_NAME[c]
    rows.append(s)
pd.DataFrame(rows)[["country", "nodf", "null_mean", "z", "p", "fill", "shape"]]
```

Every country is significantly more nested than its degree-preserving null. The matrices
are not highly nested in absolute terms, but they are consistently ordered rather than
random: complex destinations recruit across the whole education range, simpler ones from
a narrowing subset near their own level. This is the structure that lets a single
complexity axis summarise each location.
